coredata.dc:title: Effect of temperature on synthesis of carbon quantum dots and biochar through one-step hydrothermal treatment of distillers’ grains

coredata.dc:description: In seeking new pathways for the efficient utilization and high-value conversion of distillers’ grains (DG), this study adopted a hydrothermal method to process the material. The aim was to simultaneously prepare fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) and biochar (BC), while thoroughly exploring the impact of hydrothermal temperature on the luminescent properties of the CDs and the burning characteristics of the BC. The results indicated that the average particle size of the CDs produced at different hydrothermal carbonization temperatures ranged from 1.78 to 2.80 nm, with a relatively uniform particle size distribution and excellent storage stability. The QY of CDs gradually increases with the rise in hydrothermal temperature, reaching its peak at 220°C, with a value of 7.23 %. Under ultraviolet excitation at 435 nm, the CDs emitted blue fluorescence, and their emission spectra exhibited a dependency on the excitation wavelength. The CDs prepared at a hydrothermal carbonization temperature of 220 °C demonstrated the highest fluorescence intensity and exhibited a high specificity for Fe3 +, showing a linear range between 100 and 2000 μM. As the hydrothermal carbonization reaction temperature increased, the yield of BC decreased; however, at a reaction temperature of 220 °C, its HHV reached its peak. The combustion process of the BC was divided into three stages, with the decomposition and combustion of cellulose being the primary weight loss phase, demonstrating good combustion stability and sustainability, showcasing potential as a high-quality fuel.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In seeking new pathways for the efficient utilization and high-value conversion of distillers’ grains (DG), this study adopted a hydrothermal method to process the material. The aim was to simultaneously prepare fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) and biochar (BC), while thoroughly exploring the impact of hydrothermal temperature on the luminescent properties of the CDs and the burning characteristics of the BC. The results indicated that the average particle size of the CDs produced at different hydrothermal carbonization temperatures ranged from 1.78 to 2.80 nm, with a relatively uniform particle size distribution and excellent storage stability. The QY of CDs gradually increases with the rise in hydrothermal temperature, reaching its peak at 220°C, with a value of 7.23 %. Under ultraviolet excitation at 435 nm, the CDs emitted blue fluorescence, and their emission spectra exhibited a dependency on the excitation wavelength. The CDs prepared at a hydrothermal carbonization temperature of 220 °C demonstrated the highest fluorescence intensity and exhibited a high specificity for Fe3 +, showing a linear range between 100 and 2000 μM. As the hydrothermal carbonization reaction temperature increased, the yield of BC decreased; however, at a reaction temperature of 220 °C, its HHV reached its peak. The combustion process of the BC was divided into three stages, with the decomposition and combustion of cellulose being the primary weight loss phase, demonstrating good combustion stability and sustainability, showcasing potential as a high-quality fuel.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, as the consumption capacity of Chinese liquor continued to grow, the production of distillers’ grains (DG) has been gradually increasing. According to statistics, China's annual DG production is estimated at around 24 million tons (Feng et al., 2024; Xu et al., 2015). DG are byproducts of the brewing process and are rich in protein, carbohydrates, and various inorganic elements, making them highly valuable for development and utilization. Currently, most manufacturers mainly handle DG by simply drying and selling it as animal feed, while some even opt for direct landfilling (Cai et al., 2024; Xu et al., 2015). However, due to their high moisture and acidity content, DG is susceptible to decay and bacterial growth, resulting in the squandering of resources and environmental pollution (Arslanoğlu and Tümen, 2021). Therefore, the rational use of DG holds significant implications for resource utilization and environmental protection in China. With the implementation of China's "dual carbon" strategy and the urgent need for the green development of DG, the existing traditional processing methods are struggling to meet the required standards. Thus, it’s crucial to explore a novel processing approach that is harmless and resource-efficient, while also capable of large-scale operation and standardization.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs), a new type of carbon nanomaterial with fluorescent properties, mainly consist of a carbon core and organic functional groups attached to the surface. CDs have been widely employed in various fields such as ion detection, bioimaging, cell labeling, optoelectronic devices, and fluorescent tagging. In 2004, Xu et al. (2004) accidentally discovered CDs as a byproduct while preparing single-walled carbon nanotubes, marking the first discovery of CDs. The methods for synthesizing CDs mainly include hydrothermal synthesis, microwave-assisted, laser ablation, and electrochemical methods, among others (Barrientos et al., 2023; Manikandan and Lee, 2022). Among these, hydrothermal synthesis is commonly preferred for its straightforward nature and economic benefits. In recent years, the use of waste materials to produce CDs has emerged as an innovative approach for their high-value utilization, prompting many researchers to explore various waste sources for resource recycling. Studies have shown that numerous waste materials, such as bio-waste lignin, coffee grounds, fruit peels, and discarded paper (Han et al., 2024; Park et al., 2020; Shi et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2024), can serve as raw materials for synthesizing CDs through hydrothermal methods. For instance, some researchers have successfully extracted CDs from kiwi fruit peel, which exhibit excellent fluorescent properties and find extensive applications in bioimaging and sensor technologies (Atchudan et al., 2022, 2021).

In these studies, N-doped CDs have attracted considerable interest due to their improved optical characteristics. Nitrogen doping not only increases the fluorescence intensity of CDs but also improves their electrochemical performance, thereby enhancing their potential applications in sensors and optoelectronic devices (Dey et al., 2013). As a nitrogen- and organic-rich waste material, DG may serve as an excellent raw material for the synthesis of N-doped CDs. Furthermore, ethanol has been documented to serve a significant function in the hydrothermal surface passivation of CDs, leading to enhancements in their luminescence characteristics, water solubility, as well as the overall uniformity and excellence of the CDs (Shukla et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2023). Therefore, the residual ethanol in DG may play a facilitating role in the synthesis process, further enhancing the application performance of CDs.

Hydrothermal carbonization for producing CDs simultaneously generates BC, which possesses numerous advantages and finds widespread applications in various fields. Many studies indicate that the temperature of the hydrothermal reaction is one of the key factors influencing the performance of CDs and BC (Ji et al., 2024; Xie et al., 2017). A rise in temperature typically accelerates the reaction rate, promoting the decomposition and transformation of organic materials, thereby affecting the properties of the final products. Zhang et al. (2016) discovered a substantial enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of CDs as the hydrothermal temperature rose. In addition, higher hydrothermal temperature was proved to improve the specific surface area and porous structure of BC, thereby enhancing its adsorption performance and electrochemical activity (Tian et al., 2022). Specifically, higher temperatures help increase the carbonization degree of organic materials, thereby improving the physicochemical properties of BC (Poomsawat and Poomsawat, 2021). Temperature variations significantly affect the conversion rate of organic materials and the properties of the final BC when using different raw materials (such as sawdust and vegetable waste) for BC preparation (Huang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2022). Therefore, a deeper investigation into the effects of hydrothermal temperature on CDs and BC will help reveal the potential application value of these materials, providing new insights for promoting the high-value utilization of biomass resources.

In this study, the goal was to utilize DG as a carbon source to successfully prepare CDs with blue fluorescence characteristics and BC with excellent combustion performance through hydrothermal methods. To explore the properties of these products in depth, various characterization techniques were utilized to investigate the structure and optical characteristics of CDs, along with the functional groups, structure, pore size, and thermal properties of BC, assessing its potential as biomass fuel. Furthermore, the metal ion sensing characteristics of CDs were studied, revealing that they exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity towards iron ions (Fe3+) in aqueous solutions among various metal ions. This not only provided a new pathway for the resource utilization of DG but also offered references for the preparation and application of CDs and BC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous preparation of CDs and BC
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence CDs and BC were simultaneously synthesized by simple one-step hydrothermal method, as shown in Fig. 1. Briefly, twenty grams of DG (moisture content 58 %, sourced from Zunyi, Guizhou Province) were weighed and mixed thoroughly in 30 g of ultrapure water. The blend was introduced into a polytetrafluoroethylene reaction container and underwent reactions at precise temperatures (160°C, 180°C, 200°C, 220°C) for 5 hours. After the completion of the reaction, the vessel was cooled to ambient temperature. Subsequently, the resulting product underwent centrifugation at 10,000 revolutions per minute for 20 minutes and was filtered twice through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The solid carbon portion was dried at 105 °C until a constant weight was achieved. The BC prepared at different hydrothermal temperatures was named BC-X, where X is defined as 160, 180, 200, and 220. The liquid carbon fraction underwent a 24-hour dialysis process employing a 500 Da dialysis bag to isolate CDs, which were then freeze-dried to yield carbon quantum dot powder, and preserved in a refrigerator at 4 °C for future applications. The CDs prepared at different hydrothermal temperatures were named CDs-X, where X is defined as 160, 180, 200, and 220.

To study the selectivity of CDs for different metal ions, solutions of various metal ions (K⁺, Na⁺, Fe³⁺, Ca²⁺, Co²⁺, Fe²⁺, Zn²⁺, Mg²⁺, Al³⁺, Cu²⁺, Mn²⁺) were crafted at a concentration of 250 μM. Each salt solution was individually combined with one milliliter of a 50 μg/ml CDs-220 solution. After a 5-minute static reaction, the mixtures were evaluated using a fluorescence spectrophotometer, with measurements taken at the ideal excitation wavelength of 358 nm. Additionally, the study also involved investigating the correlation between fluorescence intensity and ion concentration to better understand the impact of specific metal ions on CDs within a concentration range of 0–2000 μM.

To investigate the impact of anions on the fluorescence properties of CDs-220 solutions, solutions of various anions (Cl-, CO3 2-, HCO3 -, NO3 -, SO4 2-, SO3 2-, NO2 -, and HPO3 2-) were prepared at a concentration of 250 μM. Each solution was then mixed with 1 ml of 50 μg/ml CDs-220 solution. Following a 5-minute static reaction, the mixtures were evaluated using a fluorescence spectrophotometer, with measurements taken at an ideal excitation wavelength of 358 nm.

The observation of CDs was conducted utilizing a Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) (F200 High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope, JEOL, Japan). The composition of CDs was examined through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (ESCALAB 250Xi K-Alpha, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The functional groups of CDs and BC were analyzed using a Fourier infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (INVENIO S, Bruker, Germany). The CD's optical characteristics were examined and assessed through UV–visible absorption spectroscopy (MAPADA P3, China) and fluorescence spectrophotometry (FLUORO 9 PLUS, China). The acidity and alkalinity of CDs were measured using a pH meter (PB-10, Sartorius, Germany). The automated elemental analysis instrument (Vario EL III, Elementar, Germany) was utilized to determine the elemental composition of BC. The Thermal Gravimetric Analyzer (TG) (STA449, NETZSCH, Germany) was employed to determine the thermal weight loss characteristics of BC in an oxygen atmosphere. The surface area analyzer (ASAP 2460, Micromeritics, USA) was employed to investigate the surface and adsorption properties of BC. The HHV of BC was determined through the utilization of the formula:
HHV = 0.3491 C + 1.1783H + 0.105S − 0.1034 O − 0.0151 N − 0.0211Ash.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of quantum yield of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield (QY) of CDs was determined by referencing previous experiments (Mu et al., 2024). It was measured relative to the benchmark value of quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution (known quantum yield of 54 %) using a reference method. The calculation for the relative QY is expressed as shown in the formula:
QY
CDs
=
QY ST
(
k CDs
/
k ST
) (
η CDs
/
η ST
)
2

The formula incorporates several key variables: QY symbolizes the quantum yield, k signifies the slopes derived from linear regression curves analyzing the integrated fluorescence intensity-absorption across various concentrations, η denotes the solvent's refractive index, while CDs and ST refer to CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. Of particular note is the ratio η CDs/η ref = 1.0.

The morphologies of CDs synthesized from DG at four different temperatures were examined using TEM. As depicted in Fig. 2(a, b, c, d), the CDs synthesized at all four temperatures exhibited clear and distinct lattice spacings, indicating that the prepared CDs had high crystallinity. Specifically, CDs-160 and CDs-200 formed a continuous crystal phase with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, while CDs-180 displayed a spacing of 0.18 nm, and CDs-220 exhibited a spacing of 0.20 nm. The values align with the lattice parameters of the (100) plane in standard graphite configurations (Li et al., 2019; Siahcheshm et al., 2023). The differences in lattice spacing suggested that the CDs exhibited random orientations (Liu et al., 2020). By calculating the average particle size of the CDs, represented in Fig. 2(e, f, g, h), it was found that the particle size gradually decreased with increasing hydrothermal temperature, with average sizes ranging from 1.78 to 2.80 nm. However, at higher temperatures, increased particle collisions and aggregation within the reaction system led to a larger particle size for CDs-200 (Zhang et al., 2016). The homogeneous organic structures were derived from the initial process of desiccation, interconnection, and formation of DG polymers (dos Santos et al., 2021; Titirici and Antonietti, 2010). The CDs obtained displayed high solubility in water, yielding a steadfast brownish-yellow clear aqueous solution that remained stable even following extended periods of centrifugation and filtration.

Using CDs-220 as an example, XPS was employed to characterize its composition and bonding configuration, as shown in Fig. 3. The composition of the CDs, as indicated by the prominent presence of C, O, and N, appeared to originate predominantly from proteins, amino acids, and various constituents within the DG (Fig. 3a). The high-resolution XPS analysis of C, as illustrated in Fig. 3(b), exhibited two distinct peaks at 285.1 eV and 287.5 eV, providing evidence for the existence of C-C/CC and CO chemical bonds. The observation also signified a notable existence of graphite formations and a profusion of chemical entities present on the surface of the CDs (Zhang et al., 2023b). The detailed XPS analysis of oxygen as illustrated in Fig. 3(c) exhibited distinct peaks at 532.3 eV and 531.0 eV, indicating the presence of C-O-C/C-OH and CO chemical bonds, respectively. Additionally, the N1s spectral lines were observed at 400.2 eV and 402.1 eV, which could be attributed to two nitrogen atom bonding forms, N-CO and N-H. The XPS spectra confirmed that the CDs prepared from DG possessed a rich variety of oxygen-based functional moieties (C-O-C, C-OH, CO, among others), thereby enhancing their water solubility and stability in aqueous solutions, which further facilitated subsequent functionalization and practical applications.

FTIR spectroscopy was additionally employed to examine the surface chemical moieties present on both the DG and the CDs synthesized under various temperature conditions, as illustrated in Fig. 4(a). The surface functional groups of the CDs synthesized at the four temperatures were generally consistent, with only slight variations in relative abundance. Both the DG and CDs displayed a broad hydroxyl characteristic peak at 3415 cm⁻¹ , while the DG exhibited a prominent characteristic peak at 2925 cm⁻¹ , likely related to C-H stretching vibrations (Hu et al., 2020). A distinct characteristic peak appeared at 1595 cm⁻¹ , whose intensity gradually increased with higher hydrothermal temperatures, potentially associated with CO stretching vibrations in the amide I band (Jian et al., 2017). Peaks falling within the spectral range of 1231 cm⁻¹ to 1512 cm⁻¹ may potentially signify the manifestation of C-N stretching vibrations associated with aromatic amines (Travlou et al., 2018). The peaks at 1350, 1354, and 1184 cm⁻¹ in the CDs also indicated the formation of aromatic amine structures (Liu et al., 2023), suggesting the presence of sp² structures enriched in graphene within the CDs, which aligned with the TEM results. Typically, when aromatic structures form, most peaks experience a redshift. Compared to the DG, many peaks in the CDs slightly shifted; thus, the band at 1037 cm⁻¹ likely resulted from C-O stretching vibrations (Hu et al., 2023), gradually diminishing with increasing hydrothermal temperature.

The photoluminescence (PL) and UV-Vis absorption spectroscopy were employed to investigate the optical characteristics of CDs synthesized under varying hydrothermal temperatures. The UV-Vis absorption spectrum of the CDs dispersed in an aqueous solution is depicted in Fig. 4(b). The spectrum displayed a peak at 279 nm, which gradually weakened with increasing reaction temperature. The observed decline in strength was thought to have been caused by the π-π* transitions of the sp² hybridized CC bonds within the CDs, suggesting the existence of conjugated double bonds and carbonyl groups on the CDs' surface (Goswami et al., 2022). This observation aligned with the outcomes obtained from the FTIR analysis. The CDs solutions prepared at different hydrothermal temperatures appeared as transparent, uniform brown liquids under visible light, but they exhibited bright blue photoluminescence when exposed to ultraviolet light at 365 nm (Fig. 4b, inset).

The fluorescence spectra of the CDs synthesized under various hydrothermal temperatures are depicted in Fig. 5. CDs-160 and CDs-180 showed optimal excitation wavelengths at 365 nm and maximum emission wavelengths at 443 nm. In contrast, CDs-200 and CDs-220 had optimal excitation wavelengths at 358 nm and maximum emission wavelengths at 437 nm. Their fluorescence emissions were excitation-dependent and exhibited good symmetry. The fluorescence intensity was noted to steadily rise as the hydrothermal temperature increased. Fig. 5(b, e, h, k) indicated that all four types of CDs displayed excitation-dependent photoluminescent behavior, which could be ascribed to the changes in particle dimensions and/or surface chemical compositions (Goswami et al., 2022). The QY of CD-160, CD-180, CD-200 and CD-220 were calculated to be 1.67 %, 2.77 %, 4.19 % and 7.23 %, respectively. It was observed that with increasing hydrothermal temperature, the CDs of CDs progressively increased, with CDs-220 reaching the highest value. This is attributed to the fact that under high-temperature conditions, the organic materials in the DG were more prone to decomposition and reorganization, resulting in the formation of smaller carbon nanoparticles, which in turn improved their fluorescent characteristics (Khan et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2016). Under high temperature conditions, the proteins, sugars, fats, and other components in DG were more prone to depolymerization. During depolymerization, these complex macromolecules break down into smaller radicals or fragments that form the core structure of CDs (Kumar Pandey et al., 2025). After depolymerization, these small organic molecules were enriched by reactive groups dehydration, condensation, cross-linking, and carbonization under high temperature and pressure to form CDs, which further evolved into CDs with excellent fluorescence properties (Liu et al., 2024; Zhu et al., 2024). Based on the emission data related to the blue fluorescence of the CDs received under optimal excitation, International Commission on Illumination (CIE) coordinates were further obtained, as shown in Fig. 5(c, f, i, l).

Generally, the fluorescence stability of the CDs is particularly important for their widespread application. Therefore, the fluorescence stability of the prepared CDs was evaluated through the continuous monitoring of the fluorescence intensity of the four different CDs solutions over storage time, as well as the changes in pH over storage time. The corresponding results are shown in Fig. 6. The pH of the CDs exhibited a slight decreasing trend with increasing hydrothermal temperature (Fig. 6a). This reduction in pH observed can be attributed to the generation of additional oxygen-containing functional groups at higher temperatures (Kaur et al., 2022), which enhanced the acidity of the CDs in aqueous solutions. These results were consistent with the FTIR results. The fluorescence intensity remained stable over a storage period of approximately 36 days, and the pH also remained relatively unchanged throughout the long-term storage. These results demonstrated that the prepared CDs exhibited good stability, opening the door for low-cost mass production and further practical applications of CDs derived from DG waste.

The four types of CDs prepared at different hydrothermal temperatures all exhibit strong fluorescent properties, good stability, and water solubility, in addition to featuring a diverse array of surface functional groups, rendering them highly viable for sensing applications. Therefore, this study systematically explored the feasibility of using CDs, specifically CDs-220, as sensors for detecting metal ions in water bodies. Twelve common metal ions (K⁺, Ca²⁺, Fe³⁺, Co²⁺, Ni²⁺, Zn²⁺, Cu²⁺, Na⁺, Mn²⁺, Fe²⁺, Mg²⁺, and Al³⁺) were specifically chosen for a study on their impact on the fluorescence levels of CDs solutions when subjected to ideal excitation conditions. The bar graph depicted in Fig. 7(a) illustrated the comparison of fluorescence intensity (I/I₀, where I represents the fluorescence intensity of CDs subsequent to the introduction of metal ions and I₀ denotes the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence of metal ions) following the addition of different metal ions. The presence of Fe³⁺ notably suppressed the fluorescence of the CDs in comparison to the other metal ions. The findings suggest that CDs exhibit a greater attraction towards Fe³⁺ ions, leading to a quicker chelation process. The reduction in fluorescence intensity of CDs caused by Fe³⁺ could be attributed to the development of a non-fluorescent compound due to the chelation of Fe³⁺ with oxygen (or nitrogen) groups located on the CDs' surface. This process disrupts radiative transitions, resulting in the observed fluorescence quenching of the CDs (Chen et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2020). These results demonstrated that the prepared CDs had high selectivity and stable responses for detecting Fe³⁺ in aqueous solutions.

Furthermore, the importance of the CDs in effectively identifying Fe³⁺ is essential for their real-world utilization. The detection range of CDs-220 solutions was assessed by analyzing the variations in fluorescence intensity when different concentrations of Fe³⁺ were introduced, as illustrated in Fig. 7(b). Notably, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs solution exhibited a progressive decrease with the elevated presence of Fe³⁺, suggesting a discerning responsiveness of the CDs towards varying Fe³⁺ concentrations. Fig. 7(c) showed a strong linear correlation between the concentration of Fe³⁺ and the intensity of fluorescence, represented by the equations: I/I₀ = -6.3782 C + 4483.6 (for 100–400 μM, R² = 0.9973) and I/I₀ = -0.7373 C + 2161.7 (for 400–2000 μM, R² = 0.9945). The detection limit (LOD) was established at 4.49 μM, calculated as LOD = 3σ/s, with σ representing the standard deviation of the signal and s denoting the slope of the linear calibration line. The determined LOD was comparable to or even superior to results presented in previous literature ( Table 1). Notably, this measurement complies with the specified standard for iron in drinking water by the World Health Organization, set at 5.4 µM (Jeong et al., 2023; Siahcheshm et al., 2023). This result suggested that the prepared CDs could serve as optical sensors for accurately measuring iron ions in water-based solutions.

To verify the selectivity of CDs-220 towards Fe³⁺, the study investigated the influence of several anions (Cl⁻, CO₃²⁻, HCO₃⁻, NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻, SO₃²⁻, HPO₃²⁻, and NO₂⁻) on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-220 solution. As shown in Fig. 7(d), the fluorescence intensity remained relatively stable after the addition of the anions to the CDs-220 solution, indicating that the anions in the metal salts did not interfere with the experimental results. This also suggested that CDs-220 exhibits unique selectivity for Fe³⁺. Competitive experiments were carried out using CDs-220 to assess its selectivity towards Fe³⁺ ions. The results, presented in Fig. 7(e), showed that 250 μM of Fe³⁺ was added to a CDs-220 aqueous solution containing equal concentrations of Zn²⁺, Co²⁺, Ni²⁺, Al³⁺, Cu²⁺, Mg²⁺, Mn²⁺, Ca²⁺, Na⁺, and K⁺. The response of CDs-220 to Fe³⁺ remained largely unchanged before and after the addition of the other interfering metal ions. These findings supported the potential of CDs-220 as an effective fluorescence probe for the precise and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions in aqueous solutions.

This cost-effective and efficient approach has the ability to generate a specific quantity of BC during the preparation of CDs. BC mainly derived from the carbonization of cellulose materials in DG following dehydration and decarboxylation processes. Additionally, during the carbonization process, soluble sugars and sugars produced from the decomposition of cellulose in DG underwent polymerization to form carbon particles (Jian et al., 2017). The rise in hydrothermal temperature resulted in heightened decomposition of structural cellulose materials, causing a reduction in the production of BC, as indicated in Table 2.

The elevation of hydrothermal temperature resulted in a progressive increment in the oxygen levels of BC. This increase was due to the partial oxidation of organic materials in the DG at higher hydrothermal temperatures (Zhang et al., 2023a, 2016). As the hydrothermal temperature rose, the evaporation and removal rate of moisture accelerated, reducing the moisture content in the DG and relatively increasing the carbon and hydrogen content in BC. Concurrently, the decomposition and carbonization degree of organic materials increased, leading to the removal or decomposition of water and some volatile components of low molecular weight organic substances, which enriched the remaining solid carbon with carbon elements and contributed to the increase in HHV (Travlou et al., 2018). Therefore, with the rise in hydrothermal temperature, the HHV of BC exhibited an increasing trend, reaching a peak of 27.9 MJ/kg at 220 °C, which met the high heating value standards for high-rank coal as specified by China’s national standard (GB/T 15224.3–2004). Compared to other biochar, this HHV was higher. For instance, BC derived from waste biomass had an HHV of 22.7–27.7 MJ/kg (Holliday et al., 2022); BC obtained from co-hydrothermal treatment of rapeseed straw and microalgae had an HHV of 22.30 MJ/kg (Liu et al., 2022); and BC derived from co-carbonization of digested sludge and cow manure digestate exhibited an HHV of 17.57 MJ/kg (Xu et al., 2019).

FTIR analysis was conducted to examine alterations in surface functional groups of DG and BC synthesized under different hydrothermal temperatures, depicted in Fig. 8. The absorption peak observed at 3400 cm⁻¹ was most likely associated with the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl (-OH) groups. The hydrothermal carbonization process resulted in dehydration reactions (Liang et al., 2019), which intensified the decomposition of organic materials in the DG with rising hydrothermal temperatures, thereby increasing the production of -OH groups. The absorption peak at 2900 cm⁻¹ was associated with the stretching vibrations of -CH groups (Zhang et al., 2023b). As the hydrothermal treatment deepened, the broadband at 1023 cm⁻¹ related to C-O-C gradually disappeared. This disappearance could be attributed to the ongoing breakdown and disintegration of hemicellulose and cellulose frameworks within the derived gas during the process of hydrothermal carbonization. Consequently, this process resulted in the formation of BC, CDs, and various other liquid-phase compounds. Additionally, with the increase in preparation temperature, the decomposition rate accelerated, and the degree of decomposition deepened (Hu et al., 2023). The intense peak observed at 1590 cm⁻¹ corresponds to the stretching vibration of CO, which could be related to the decarboxylation reactions occurring during hydrothermal carbonization (Shabbir et al., 2022). The characteristic peak at 1357 cm⁻¹ possibly correlated with the bending vibrations of C-H or the deformation vibrations of carbon structures (Mohapatra et al., 2023). As the hydrothermal carbonization temperature increased, more polymerization and crosslinking reactions occurred, resulting in increased complexity of the carbon structure. This structural change likely enriched the chemical environment associated with C-H bending vibrations, thereby enhancing the absorption peak in that region (Wang et al., 2023).

Fig. 8(b) and (c) present the TG and DTG curves of DG and BC obtained at various hydrothermal temperatures. As depicted in Fig. 8(b), the TGA curves of BC prepared at different temperatures initially exhibited a gradual decline. However, as the temperature rose, they began to drop sharply until reaching 600 °C, after which the curves stabilized again and displayed a gentle trend. The variations in the TGA curves of BC prepared at different hydrothermal temperatures could be divided into three stages. During Stage I, there was a gradual decline in sample weight attributed to the process of moisture evaporation, occurring within the temperature range of 33–237 °C (Hu et al., 2023). During Stage II (237–493 °C), a notable decrease in sample mass occurred as a result of the evaporation and combustion of volatile components, stemming from the decomposition of cellulose and hemicellulose structures in the DG decomposed upon heating (Poomsawat and Poomsawat, 2021). Stage III showed that the sample mass was relatively stable, indicating that fixed carbon was burning, and the decomposition of lignin resulted in a decrease in mass, occurring between temperature range of 493–800 °C (de Almeida et al., 2022).

Taking BC-160 as an example, the first weight loss process exhibited a peak at 228 °C (0.20434 %/°C), which was the smallest peak and corresponded to the first stage of the TGA curve, resulting from the volatilization caused by hydrolysis. The second weight loss process showed a peak at 292 °C (1.30621 %/°C), which was the largest peak and fell within the second stage of the TGA curve, attributed to the rapid volatilization and combustion of volatile components. The third weight loss process had a peak at 453 °C (0.28043 %/°C), located in the third stage of the TGA curve. This peak was higher than that of the first stage but lower than that of the second stage, a phenomenon that was attributed to the combustion of fixed carbon.

As the hydrothermal temperature increased, the cooling stage corresponding to the TGA curves gradually shifted to the right, indicating that higher preparation temperatures made the combustion of BC more challenging. The DTG curves of BC prepared at different temperatures are shown in Fig. 8(c), revealing three distinct peaks during the combustion process, corresponding to three weight-loss events (Liu et al., 2023). As the preparation temperature of BC increased, the peak corresponding to the volatiles decreased continuously. Therefore, with the rise in preparation temperature for BC, the temperature at which the weight loss rate peak appeared also increased, suggesting that higher hydrothermal temperatures favored the combustion of fixed carbon, leading to more complete combustion of BC (Wang et al., 2024; Zhi et al., 2024).

ce:section.ce:section-title: BET and pore structural characteristics of BC
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 illustrated the nitrogen adsorption-desorption curves of BC generated under varying hydrothermal temperatures. The BC generated at varying temperatures exhibited type I isotherms at lower relative pressures and type IV isotherms at higher relative pressures, suggesting a blend of type I and type IV characteristics (Mo et al., 2020). The adsorption and desorption isotherms observed for the four variations of BC showed a slow increase in the low P/P0 region, demonstrating minimal adsorption at lower relative pressures; the adsorption-desorption curves overlapped, indicating that nitrogen adsorption was primarily monolayer adsorption at this stage (Onorevoli et al., 2018). Within the pressure range of 0.2–0.8, the isotherm gradually increased, indicating multilayer adsorption; between 0.8 and 1.0, the adsorption and desorption curves exhibited a noticeable H4-type hysteresis loop. This phenomenon was attributed to the capillary condensation of nitrogen within the mesopores, where the mesopore morphology presented layered and slit-like non-uniform channels (Huang and Doong, 2012). This finding indicated that the multi-layered bundled structure of the DG was retained within the BC, with the pores primarily consisting of micropores and mesopores.

The pore structure parameter data for BC produced at various hydrothermal temperatures was presented in Table 3. The BET value of BC prepared at different temperatures were only 3.33–8.82 m2/g, which was higher than that of the biochar prepared by Wu et al. (2022) with lignin (0.62–1.32 m2/g) and aligning closely with the findings of Ibrahim et al. (2021) using Oil Palm Empty Fruit Bunch (3.05–3.78 m2/g). Nevertheless, it is crucial to note that the efficacy of heavy metal adsorption is not solely dictated by the precise surface area (Cao et al., 2019; Fan et al., 2020). With the increase in hydrothermal temperature, the specific surface area, mean pore diameter, and overall pore volume exhibited a trend of first increasing, then decreasing, and subsequently increasing again. This pattern occurred because the organic materials in the DG decomposed and partially carbonized at lower hydrothermal temperatures, leading to a significant formation of micropore structures, which increased both the pore volume and BET specific surface area (Guo et al., 2017). As the temperature further increased, organic materials underwent a certain degree of carbonization and polymerization reactions, which caused further evolution of the surface and pore structure of the BC. This evolution led to the partial closure or reduction of pores, resulting in decreased specific surface area and pore volume (Zhang et al., 2021). When the temperature reached a certain level, the skeletal structure of the BC underwent reorganization, forming larger cavities or porous structures, which subsequently increased both the specific surface area and pore volume again (Cai et al., 2016). Additionally, higher temperatures might have facilitated further carbonization of the carbon, leading to the formation of more stable porous materials.

In summary, this study utilized a simple one-step hydrothermal method to simultaneously synthesize fluorescence CDs and BC from DG, exploring the effects of hydrothermal temperature on the properties of both CDs and BC. This research provided a scientific basis for optimizing preparation processes. The CDs produced at four different hydrothermal temperatures exhibited small and uniform particle sizes. Among them, CDs-220 had the smallest average particle size of 1.78 nm, while CDs-180 demonstrated the lowest lattice spacing at 0.18 nm. The QY of CDs gradually increases with the rise in hydrothermal temperature, reaching its peak at 220°C, with a value of 7.23 %. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs steadily rose as the temperature increased, with CDs-220 exhibiting the most pronounced fluorescence intensity. At the ideal excitation wavelength, the CDs at all four temperatures displayed blue fluorescence emission characteristics along with excitation-dependent photoluminescence phenomena and high fluorescence durability. The assay demonstrated exceptional selectivity towards Fe3+ ions, showing a detection range from 100 to 2000 μM and a LOD of 4.49 μM.

The rise in temperature led to a gradual decline in the yield of BC. Nonetheless, the high temperatures facilitated the decomposition of cellulose, resulting in the increase of HHV of BC. The HHV of BC-220 reached 27.9 MJ/kg, demonstrating good stability and continuity during combustion, indicating its potential to serve as a high-quality fuel. The surface area specific to BET analysis, cumulative volume of pores, and mean pore size of BC exhibited a trend of initially increasing, then decreasing, and subsequently increasing again with the rise in hydrothermal temperature. This indicated that BC has potential application value in the field of adsorption and may serve as an effective adsorbent, warranting further attention. Both the high selectivity of the CDs for metal ion detection and the valuable fuel characteristics of BC highlighted their promising applications. This research contributes reference and ideas for the high-value conversion and diversified utilization of waste DG.

Wu Wanting: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Luo Xinyu: Writing – original draft, Visualization. Mu Lan: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Huang Jialiang: Writing – review & editing. Zhang Yanhong: Writing – review & editing. Tao Junyu: Writing – review & editing. Peng Hao: Writing – review & editing.
